case_A: A person gives disorganized feedback on a friend's manuscript, mixing up some details but clearly having read every word and cared about the story.
case_B: A person gives professional, articulate feedback on a friend's manuscript, carefully avoiding anything that might feel personal.
notes: Professional distance vs messy engagement

case_A: A teacher assigns perfectly structured homework that fits curriculum standards but shows no adaptation to what students are actually struggling with.
case_B: A teacher throws together a somewhat disorganized review sheet that directly addresses the errors students made on the last test.
notes: Standards-compliant but inattentive vs messy but responsive

case_A: A family member gives a halting, emotional speech at a relative's graduation dinner that goes slightly long but is full of specific memories.
case_B: A family member sends a beautifully written letter for a relative's graduation that is clearly a generic template adapted for the occasion.
notes: Polished template vs heartfelt ramble

case_A: A project lead gives polished status updates to stakeholders that make everything sound on track, while ignoring team concerns about the schedule.
case_B: A project lead gives an unpolished and slightly alarming status update that honestly conveys the team's concerns about the schedule, risking stakeholder panic.
notes: Polished cover-up vs rough honesty

case_A: A manager writes a polished diversity and inclusion statement for the company but does not change any hiring practices.
case_B: A manager awkwardly expands the hiring pipeline to include overlooked candidates, making some missteps in outreach but genuinely broadening the pool.
notes: Polished statement without action vs imperfect but genuine change

case_A: A community organizer runs a perfectly executed town hall but controls the microphone so only aligned speakers are heard.
case_B: A community member runs a slightly chaotic open forum where everyone gets to speak, even if some comments go off-topic.
notes: Controlled event vs chaotic but open event

case_A: A colleague says a brief, slightly abrupt goodbye to the departing coworker but had consistently helped them with day-to-day work over the past year.
case_B: A colleague writes a beautiful farewell message when a coworker leaves, though they never once helped that person during their time together.
notes: Eloquent farewell without substance vs abrupt goodbye after real help

case_A: A person delivers a smooth, practiced apology after hurting a friend, hitting all the right notes without genuinely reflecting on what they did.
case_B: A person delivers a stumbling, heartfelt apology after hurting a friend, clearly struggling to find words but visibly affected by what happened.
notes: Practiced but hollow apology vs raw but genuine one

case_A: A student who did extensive research gives a rough, nervous presentation that does not do their preparation justice.
case_B: A student gives a smooth group presentation while privately having done the least amount of work.
notes: Smooth surface / low effort vs rough surface / high effort

case_A: A new employee follows the dress code perfectly and says all the right things in meetings but shows no initiative and avoids extra effort.
case_B: A new employee sometimes arrives looking slightly rumpled and speaks too directly in meetings but takes on tasks others avoid and follows through on every commitment.
notes: Polished surface / minimal effort vs rough surface / strong follow-through

case_A: A classmate lends their notes to a struggling student but tells them exactly how to study, implying the student is incapable of figuring it out alone.
case_B: A classmate offers to explain a concept to a struggling student but ends up confusing them more before ultimately helping them reach understanding through trial and error.
notes: Condescending help vs fumbled but respectful collaboration

case_A: A coworker sends a rough weekly status report that honestly includes where things went wrong and what they are doing to fix it.
case_B: A coworker sends a flawlessly formatted weekly status report that consistently omits their mistakes.
notes: Polished dishonesty vs rough honesty

case_A: A spouse forgets the exact anniversary date but cooks a slightly burned dinner the next day with genuine effort to make up for it.
case_B: A spouse plans a romantic anniversary dinner at a fancy restaurant and spends the evening checking work emails.
notes: Fancy but distracted vs imperfect but present

case_A: A person at a potluck brings a slightly lopsided homemade cake that took them all afternoon because they wanted to contribute something personal.
case_B: A person at a potluck brings an impressive store-bought dish and presents it as homemade.
notes: Deceptive impressive dish vs honest imperfect effort

case_A: A person calls to thank someone who helped them move, stumbling over words but clearly meaning it.
case_B: A person sends a perfectly formatted thank-you email to someone who helped them move, but it reads like a template.
notes: Polished template vs sincere stumbling

case_A: A sibling writes a touching social media post for their parent's birthday but forgot to call or visit.
case_B: A sibling who is terrible at social media calls their parent on their birthday and has a halting, slightly awkward conversation that lasts twenty minutes.
notes: Public performance vs private presence

case_A: A person is visibly uncomfortable at funerals and says little, but shows up the next week with a meal and stays to help with chores.
case_B: A person always says the right comforting thing at funerals but never visits the bereaved afterward.
notes: Polished condolence without follow-through vs uncomfortable but present after

case_A: A family member forgets cards and dates but calls out of the blue when they sense something might be wrong, even if the timing is inconvenient.
case_B: A family member always remembers to send a card for every occasion but never asks how anyone is actually doing.
notes: Reliable formality vs unreliable but genuine attentiveness

case_A: A neighbor smiles and waves at the new family on the block but never introduces themselves or offers any practical help.
case_B: A neighbor shows up at the new family's door with a casserole that is slightly overcooked, stumbling through an introduction but offering to help them find local services.
notes: Polite but empty welcome vs awkward but substantive one

case_A: A coworker smoothly takes over a struggling colleague's presentation, finishing it flawlessly without checking whether the colleague wanted help.
case_B: A coworker awkwardly interrupts a colleague's struggling presentation to add context that salvages the key point, then hands it back.
notes: Smooth takeover (possibly ego-driven) vs awkward intervention (protective intent)

case_A: A group project member produces polished slides but never responds to teammates' questions about content decisions.
case_B: A group project member produces rough slides but actively discusses content decisions with the team, changing their work based on feedback.
notes: Polished but unresponsive vs rough but collaborative

case_A: A parent keeps their child's schedule light, enduring judgment from other parents, because they can see the child needs more unstructured time to play and rest.
case_B: A parent signs up their child for every enrichment activity available, driven by anxiety about the child falling behind peers.
notes: Anxiety-driven over-scheduling vs child-responsive restraint

case_A: A supervisor has a blunt conversation with a struggling team member about the performance gap, risking an awkward relationship but trying to give the person a real chance to improve.
case_B: A supervisor assigns a struggling team member to easy tasks to avoid dealing with the performance issue.
notes: Avoidance disguised as accommodation vs uncomfortable honest feedback

case_A: A family member sends an expensive gift after missing several family events, without acknowledging the absences.
case_B: A family member who missed several events calls to apologize awkwardly, admitting they let other things take priority and asking to do better.
notes: Material compensation without accountability vs clumsy but honest acknowledgment

case_A: A colleague sends a well-crafted encouragement email to a teammate facing a hard deadline, but declines to help with any of the actual work.
case_B: A colleague stays late to help a teammate meet a hard deadline, working quietly in the background without saying anything particularly encouraging.
notes: Articulate but uninvolved vs inarticulate but actively helpful

case_A: A volunteer at a charity gala charms donors with witty conversation but skips the actual setup and teardown work.
case_B: A volunteer at a charity gala spends the entire day doing setup and teardown, barely speaking to anyone but making sure everything runs.
notes: Charming but unhelpful vs quiet but essential

case_A: A coworker who rarely expresses sentiment gives honest notice when they start considering other positions, even though the timing is awkward.
case_B: A coworker articulates their loyalty to the team beautifully in an email thread but quietly applies for other jobs without telling anyone.
notes: Eloquent disloyalty vs awkward honesty

case_A: A neighbor leaves a note with a phone number and drops off meals for a week after hearing about a family's loss, misspelling the family's name on the card.
case_B: A neighbor offers a perfectly worded condolence when they hear about a family's loss but never follows up or helps with practical matters.
notes: Perfect condolence with no follow-up vs imperfect but sustained practical help

case_A: A parent fumbles through advice for their teenager about a friendship problem, getting flustered but clearly invested in helping the teen think it through.
case_B: A parent gives polished, diplomatic advice to their teenager about a friendship problem, careful not to get emotionally involved.
notes: Polished detachment vs clumsy engagement

case_A: A community member volunteers a few hours at a shelter each month, sometimes showing up late or forgetting tasks, but consistently present.
case_B: A community member writes a polished op-ed about local homelessness but has never volunteered at or donated to any local shelter.
notes: Polished words without action vs imperfect but consistent action

case_A: A person checks on a friend after a breakup with a short message offering to talk whenever they need to.
case_B: A person sends a long, supportive text after a friend's breakup, but mainly because they have romantic interest in the friend and see an opening.
notes: Opportunistic comfort vs straightforward support

case_A: A colleague stays late to fix a production issue and then emails the entire leadership chain about it at midnight.
case_B: A colleague stays late to fix a production issue and just logs the fix in the normal incident tracker.
notes: Broadcasting heroics vs normal documentation

case_A: A person who visited their mentor regularly during their illness says little publicly, simply attending the memorial quietly.
case_B: A person writes a glowing public tribute to their late mentor on social media, never having visited or called the mentor in their final months.
notes: Public tribute without private care vs quiet presence

case_A: A parent throws an elaborate birthday party for their child, choosing every detail to outdo the parties other parents have thrown recently.
case_B: A parent plans a simple birthday celebration at home, focusing on the games and treats the child actually asked for.
notes: Competitive party-planning vs child-centered celebration

case_A: A parent reads bedtime stories to their child a few nights a week, engaging with the story and responding to the child's questions and reactions.
case_B: A parent reads bedtime stories to their child every night, but uses the time mainly to feel they have checked the parenting box rather than connecting with the child.
notes: Routine compliance vs engaged connection (even if less frequent)

case_A: A community member spends a Saturday picking up trash in the park alone, bringing their own bags and not telling anyone.
case_B: A community leader organizes a high-profile neighborhood cleanup, using the event mainly to campaign for a local office they plan to run for.
notes: Event as campaign tool vs quiet civic care

case_A: A parent insists on paying for their adult child's wedding and then uses that financial leverage to control decisions about the guest list and venue.
case_B: A parent offers to help with their adult child's wedding and asks what kind of help would be most useful, then follows the couple's lead.
notes: Financial control vs supportive help

case_A: A person helps sort donations at a food bank on a random Tuesday when they have a free afternoon, without telling anyone.
case_B: A person volunteers at a food bank every Thanksgiving and always makes sure someone photographs them serving meals.
notes: Photographed holiday volunteering vs unpublicized random help

case_A: A student participates in a local community service project on weekends, doing the work without any particular documentation.
case_B: A student leads a group volunteering trip abroad and spends most of the time curating content for their personal brand rather than doing the actual service work.
notes: Voluntourism for branding vs local quiet service

case_A: A person drops off old clothes at a donation bin on their way to work without posting about it.
case_B: A person donates old clothes to a shelter and insists on having the shelter tag them in a social media post.
notes: Publicized donation vs quiet donation

case_A: A person quietly pays for a student's lunch account for the whole year after learning the family is struggling.
case_B: A person makes a large donation to a school and insists on a plaque with their name in the main hallway.
notes: Flashy donation for recognition vs quiet support

case_A: A manager creates a wellness committee at work and uses it mainly to appear progressive to upper leadership.
case_B: A manager notices a team member seems burned out and quietly reduces their load for a week while checking in on them.
notes: Performative wellness vs genuine individual care

case_A: A friend organizes a group gift for another friend's wedding, collecting more than needed and keeping the surplus without telling anyone.
case_B: A friend organizes a group gift for another friend's wedding, returning the extra money to contributors after purchasing the gift.
notes: Gift fund skimming vs honest handling

case_A: A person organizes a fundraiser for a friend's medical bills but negotiates a public thank-you post and expects future social capital.
case_B: A person quietly sends money to a friend's medical fund through the anonymous option.
notes: Fundraiser for social return vs anonymous help

case_A: A person offers to walk a sick neighbor's dog and uses the daily walk as an opportunity to scope out the neighbor's property.
case_B: A person offers to walk a sick neighbor's dog and just walks the dog, returning the leash to the doorknob each time.
notes: Help-as-reconnaissance vs simple help

case_A: A person invites friends over for a home-cooked meal, spending the afternoon preparing dishes they know each friend likes.
case_B: A person hosts a lavish dinner for friends, choosing the most expensive restaurant primarily to signal their financial success.
notes: Wealth display dinner vs thoughtful home-cooked meal

case_A: A friend does not throw a party but takes the birthday person to their favorite low-key spot for a quiet dinner they would actually enjoy.
case_B: A friend plans a surprise birthday party for another friend but fills the guest list with their own contacts rather than the birthday person's actual close friends.
notes: Self-serving party vs personalized celebration

case_A: A colleague always brings baked goods to the office on Mondays, but strategically distributes them to people who can influence their next performance review.
case_B: A colleague occasionally brings in snacks for the team, rotating between different treats based on what people mentioned they liked.
notes: Strategic baked goods vs genuine thoughtful snacks

case_A: A person organizes a neighborhood watch meeting but uses the platform to position themselves as the block authority and settle personal scores.
case_B: A person attends a neighborhood watch meeting and mostly listens, occasionally sharing information about local safety resources.
notes: Power-seeking organizer vs constructive participant

case_A: A business owner sponsors a local sports team and tells the coach to just use the money however the team needs it.
case_B: A business owner sponsors a local sports team and makes sure the company logo is on every jersey, banner, and social media post.
notes: Branding-heavy sponsorship vs no-strings support

case_A: A group member volunteers to edit the final report and carefully preserves each person's contributions, only fixing errors and improving flow.
case_B: A group member volunteers to edit the final report and rewrites everyone's sections in their own voice, taking effective authorship.
notes: Editorial takeover vs respectful editing

case_A: An employee informally checks in on a new hire during their first weeks, remembering how disorienting the onboarding was when they started.
case_B: An employee mentors a new hire mainly because the department head said mentoring counts toward promotion criteria.
notes: Career-driven mentoring vs empathetic informal support

case_A: A company CEO personally responds to a customer complaint on social media, knowing the public response will look good, while the underlying issue goes unfixed internally.
case_B: A customer service representative quietly escalates a complaint through internal channels and follows up until it is resolved, without any public visibility.
notes: PR-driven response vs genuine resolution

case_A: A teacher gives extra help to the student who is most behind, adjusting their schedule to make room even though the student's family has no connections.
case_B: A teacher gives extra help to a student whose parents are on the PTA and influential in the school, spending less time with other students who need it more.
notes: Strategic favoritism vs need-based attention

case_A: A family member always hosts the grandchildren for summer but uses the visits to subtly turn the kids against their other parent.
case_B: A family member hosts the grandchildren for a weekend and focuses on making it fun, staying neutral about family dynamics.
notes: Grandparent alienation vs neutral caregiving

case_A: A teacher notices individual students' progress and writes brief personal notes in their folders, acknowledging specific improvements.
case_B: A teacher creates an award ceremony for students but selects winners in a way that makes their own teaching look effective rather than based on actual student merit.
notes: Self-serving awards vs genuine individual recognition

case_A: A colleague writes a glowing peer review for a teammate, mainly so the teammate will reciprocate and write a positive review back.
case_B: A colleague gives a peer review with honest praise and a few candid suggestions, knowing the teammate can handle constructive feedback.
notes: Strategic praise-trading vs honest feedback

case_A: A relative sends expensive gifts for every occasion but never calls, writes a personal note, or visits.
case_B: A relative sends a handwritten card and occasionally calls to check in, even though they cannot afford expensive gifts.
notes: Expensive but impersonal gifts vs modest but personal connection

case_A: A neighbor spends an hour each Saturday helping kids with homework at the community center library without any business motive.
case_B: A neighbor sets up a free tutoring table at the community center and uses it mainly to recruit clients for their paid tutoring business.
notes: Free tutoring as lead generation vs genuine free help

case_A: A student leads a campus recycling initiative mainly because it qualifies them for a scholarship they want.
case_B: A student brings their own reusable containers to every meal and cleans up after others without any formal recognition.
notes: Scholarship-motivated initiative vs unrecognized consistent practice

case_A: A student joins a campus advocacy group from conviction, taking on the less visible behind-the-scenes work that nobody else wants to do.
case_B: A student joins a campus advocacy group partly from genuine conviction and partly because the leadership position looks good on their resume.
notes: Mixed: conviction + resume vs conviction + invisible labor

case_A: A neighbor helps a new family move in to be welcoming, adjusting their own weekend plans without any expectation of return.
case_B: A neighbor helps a new family move in partly to be welcoming and partly to assess what the family owns.
notes: Mixed: welcoming + surveillance vs welcoming + sacrifice

case_A: A colleague shares a job lead with a teammate from goodwill, even though the teammate's departure would leave them short-handed.
case_B: A colleague shares a job lead with a teammate partly from goodwill and partly because the teammate leaving would free up a project they want.
notes: Mixed: goodwill + self-interest vs goodwill + accepting cost

case_A: A colleague recommends a friend for a job because the friend is qualified, even though the hire might compete with them for future promotions.
case_B: A colleague recommends a friend for a job partly because the friend is qualified and partly because having an ally in the department gives them political cover.
notes: Mixed: qualified recommendation + political alliance vs qualified recommendation + accepting competition

case_A: A family member invests in a relative's business because they believe in it, accepting that the investment might not pay off and staying hands-off.
case_B: A family member invests in a relative's business partly because they believe in it and partly because they expect disproportionate returns and a say in management.
notes: Mixed: belief + control vs belief + hands-off trust

case_A: A friend offers emotional support during a crisis partly because they care and partly because they want to be seen as the reliable one in the group.
case_B: A friend offers emotional support during a crisis even though they are dealing with their own problems and have nothing to gain from the effort.
notes: Mixed: care + reputation vs care + personal cost

case_A: A friend helps another friend study for a certification out of friendship, adjusting their own schedule even though it means falling behind on their own work.
case_B: A friend helps another friend study for a certification partly out of friendship and partly because the friend passing would validate their own teaching ability.
notes: Mixed: friendship + reflected competence vs friendship + personal cost

case_A: An employee leads the company's sustainability initiative from conviction, continuing even after the initiative loses executive attention and career benefit.
case_B: An employee leads the company's sustainability initiative partly from conviction and partly because the role is visible and good for their career.
notes: Mixed: conviction + career benefit vs conviction despite lost benefit

case_A: A relative hosts holiday gatherings partly from tradition and partly because being the host means controlling the guest list and the narrative about family disputes.
case_B: A relative hosts holiday gatherings from tradition, going out of their way to invite family members who have been estranged, even though it creates tension.
notes: Mixed: tradition + control vs tradition + reconciliation effort

case_A: A student tutors younger students partly to help and partly because the tutoring hours qualify them for a service-learning requirement.
case_B: A student tutors younger students to help, continuing to show up even after they have completed their service hours requirement.
notes: Mixed: helping + requirement vs helping beyond requirement

case_A: A person picks up litter in the park partly from civic duty and partly because they are meeting someone and want to look conscientious.
case_B: A person picks up litter in the park from civic duty, doing it on their regular walk even when no one else is around.
notes: Mixed: civic duty + impression vs civic duty alone

case_A: A person checks on a sick coworker partly from genuine concern and partly because being known as caring improves their reputation at work.
case_B: A person checks on a sick coworker from genuine concern, quietly covering some of the coworker's tasks so they can rest without falling behind.
notes: Mixed: concern + reputation vs concern + quiet practical help

case_A: A parent pushes their child to practice piano partly because the child enjoys it and partly because the parent wants to brag about the child's recital performances.
case_B: A parent pushes their child to practice piano because the child asked to learn and the parent wants to support that commitment, even though the practice schedule is hard to maintain.
notes: Mixed: supporting child + bragging vs supporting child's choice + sacrifice

case_A: A teacher gives a student extra attention partly because they care and partly because the student's parent is on the school board.
case_B: A teacher gives a student extra attention because they see the student struggling, even though the student's parent has been critical of the school.
notes: Mixed: care + political advantage vs care despite political friction

case_A: A volunteer drives elderly neighbors to appointments partly out of kindness and partly because they charge for gas at a rate that turns it into a side income.
case_B: A volunteer drives elderly neighbors to appointments out of kindness, absorbing the gas cost because the neighbors are on a fixed income.
notes: Mixed: kindness + profit-taking vs kindness + absorbing cost

case_A: A colleague shares a professional contact with a coworker partly to help and partly because they expect referral favors in return.
case_B: A colleague shares a professional contact with a coworker to help, even though the introduction might not benefit them at all.
notes: Mixed: helping + expecting favors vs helping + no strings

case_A: A coworker offers to train a new employee to be helpful, making sure the new hire also meets other team members and does not become solely dependent on one person.
case_B: A coworker offers to train a new employee partly to be helpful and partly because being the trainer gives them informal authority over the new hire's first weeks.
notes: Mixed: helpfulness + authority vs helpfulness + shared integration

case_A: A friend accompanies another to a medical appointment from care, keeping the details private even when mutual friends ask about it.
case_B: A friend accompanies another to a medical appointment partly from care and partly because they want to know the diagnosis for gossip purposes.
notes: Mixed: care + gossip interest vs care + discretion

case_A: A person helps their aging parent move into assisted living out of love, even though it means they themselves have to drive much farther for visits.
case_B: A person helps their aging parent move into assisted living partly out of love and partly because they want access to the parent's house.
notes: Mixed: love + property interest vs love + personal sacrifice

case_A: A parent volunteers to coach youth sports partly because they love kids and partly because it positions their child for more playing time.
case_B: A parent volunteers to coach youth sports because they love kids, making sure playing time is distributed fairly even when their own child protests.
notes: Mixed: love for kids + favoritism vs love for kids + fairness

case_A: A student helps organize a campus fundraiser to help the cause, putting in more hours than they can comfortably spare during exam season.
case_B: A student helps organize a campus fundraiser partly to help the cause and partly to list it on their graduate school application.
notes: Mixed: cause + resume-building vs cause + personal cost

case_A: A colleague mentors a junior employee to help them grow, even though the junior's success might overshadow the mentor's own standing.
case_B: A colleague mentors a junior employee partly to help and partly to build a faction loyal to them within the department.
notes: Mixed: mentoring + faction-building vs mentoring + risking own status

case_A: A person agrees to be a reference for a coworker partly out of support and partly because they know the coworker leaving opens up a position they want.
case_B: A person agrees to be a reference for a coworker out of genuine support, even though the coworker leaving will increase their own workload significantly.
notes: Mixed: support + self-interest vs support + personal cost

case_A: A parent coaches their kid's science fair project partly to support the child and partly to relive their own academic glory days.
case_B: A parent coaches their kid's science fair project to support the child, staying up late even though the topic is outside their expertise.
notes: Mixed: support + self-gratification vs support + stretching for the child

case_A: A teacher writes a strong college recommendation because the student deserves it, taking the time to personalize each letter even though the workload is heavy.
case_B: A teacher writes a strong college recommendation partly because the student deserves it and partly because successful alumni reflect well on the teacher.
notes: Mixed: deserved + reflected glory vs deserved + extra effort

case_A: A person volunteers to manage the community garden partly to serve the neighborhood and partly because the role gives them control over plot assignments.
case_B: A person volunteers to manage the community garden to serve the neighborhood, taking the least desirable plot for themselves so newcomers get better ones.
notes: Mixed: service + control vs service + self-sacrifice

case_A: A person organizes a memorial for a deceased community member from respect, doing most of the work themselves even though it is emotionally exhausting.
case_B: A person organizes a memorial for a deceased community member partly from respect and partly because the event raises their own standing in the community.
notes: Mixed: respect + standing vs respect + personal toll

case_A: A sibling agrees to co-sign a loan for their brother partly to help and partly to hold financial power over him.
case_B: A sibling agrees to co-sign a loan for their brother to help, understanding the risk and trusting the brother's plan even though it makes them personally vulnerable.
notes: Mixed: helping + leverage vs helping + personal risk

case_A: A student leads a group project to keep things on track, accepting that their own section will be weaker because they spent time helping others.
case_B: A student leads a group project partly to learn and partly to control the outcome so they get the highest grade.
notes: Mixed: learning + grade control vs leadership + self-sacrifice

case_A: A parent attends parent-teacher conferences partly to support their child and partly to network with other parents for professional opportunities.
case_B: A parent attends parent-teacher conferences to support their child, asking detailed questions about how to help at home even though their own schedule is packed.
notes: Mixed: support + networking vs support + genuine engagement

case_A: A housemate buys shared groceries and just splits the cost evenly without tracking every item.
case_B: A housemate buys shared groceries but keeps a precise spreadsheet and brings it up whenever there is any minor disagreement.
notes: Same act (buying groceries), motive: leveraging contributions vs easy sharing

case_A: A teacher writes a strong recommendation letter for a student because they have watched the student grow and believe in their potential.
case_B: A teacher writes a strong recommendation letter for a student, expecting the student's successful parents to return the favor somehow.
notes: Same act (writing recommendation), motive: transactional vs belief in student

case_A: A colleague covers a shift for a sick teammate because they know how hard it is to find coverage at the last minute.
case_B: A colleague covers a shift for a sick teammate, noting privately that the teammate will owe them and they plan to collect.
notes: Same act (covering shift), motive: creating debt vs solidarity

case_A: A colleague organizes a farewell party for a departing teammate because they want the person to feel appreciated before they leave.
case_B: A colleague organizes a farewell party for a departing teammate mainly to position themselves as the social connector of the office.
notes: Same act (organizing farewell), motive: social capital vs appreciation

case_A: A student tutors a struggling classmate, privately expecting the classmate to owe them favors later.
case_B: A student tutors a struggling classmate because they remember how it felt to be behind and want to make it easier.
notes: Same act (tutoring), motive: transactional leverage vs empathy

case_A: A friend listens patiently to another friend's problems because they can tell the friend needs someone to really hear them.
case_B: A friend listens patiently to another friend's problems, but mainly so they can gossip about it later.
notes: Same act (listening), motive: collecting gossip vs genuine presence

case_A: A person tips generously at a restaurant only when dining with people they want to impress.
case_B: A person tips generously at a restaurant regardless of who they are with, because they know servers depend on tips.
notes: Same act (tipping), motive: impression management vs caring about servers

case_A: A person holds the door for a stranger at a store and then makes a passive-aggressive comment when the stranger does not thank them immediately.
case_B: A person holds the door for a stranger at a store and moves on without giving it a second thought.
notes: Same act (holding door), motive: expecting payback vs automatic courtesy

case_A: A relative offers to babysit so the parents can have an evening out, and spends the time playing with the children.
case_B: A relative offers to babysit, but uses the time to snoop through the parents' personal papers and messages.
notes: Same act (babysitting), motive: snooping vs genuine help

case_A: A team member volunteers to present the group's work at the conference, privately wanting to be the face of the project and minimize others' visibility.
case_B: A team member volunteers to present the group's work at the conference because no one else felt comfortable presenting and they wanted to make sure the work got seen.
notes: Same act (volunteering to present), motive: hoarding credit vs filling a gap

case_A: A family member offers to host the holiday dinner so they can control the menu, the seating, and who gets thanked in the toast.
case_B: A family member offers to host the holiday dinner because the usual host is recovering from surgery and could use a break.
notes: Same act (hosting dinner), motive: control vs relieving burden

case_A: A person sends a congratulatory message to an old friend who got promoted, genuinely happy for them even though their own career is stalled.
case_B: A person sends a congratulatory message to an old friend who got promoted, privately feeling bitter and hoping the friend will fail eventually.
notes: Same act (congratulating), motive: masked envy vs genuine happiness

case_A: A student shares their class notes online, expecting others to follow them on social media in return.
case_B: A student shares their class notes online because the professor's lectures were confusing and they figure everyone could use the help.
notes: Same act (sharing notes), motive: follower-farming vs helping classmates

case_A: A student organizes a study group for the final exam because everyone seems lost and studying together might help the whole group.
case_B: A student organizes a study group for the final exam, privately hoping the others will share their notes so the student can study less on their own.
notes: Same act (organizing study group), motive: extracting others' work vs collective benefit

case_A: A mentor checks in on a former mentee regularly because they genuinely care about how the mentee is doing and want to stay connected.
case_B: A mentor checks in on a former mentee regularly, partly because they want to be known as the person who shaped the mentee's career.
notes: Same act (mentoring check-ins), motive: legacy-building vs genuine care

case_A: A coworker stays late to help a colleague finish a report, hoping the manager will notice and give them a promotion over the colleague.
case_B: A coworker stays late to help a colleague finish a report because they know the colleague is overwhelmed and could use the support.
notes: Same act (staying late to help), motive: self-advancement vs genuine care

case_A: A supervisor offers flexible hours to an employee dealing with a family crisis because they want the employee to have the space to handle things at home.
case_B: A supervisor offers flexible hours to an employee dealing with a family crisis, expecting the employee to work harder in return.
notes: Same act (offering flexibility), motive: extracting future labor vs genuine support

case_A: A manager gives a junior employee a chance to present to the client, expecting the junior to fail and thus prove the manager is indispensable.
case_B: A manager gives a junior employee a chance to present to the client because the manager believes in developing their team's confidence.
notes: Same act (delegating presentation), motive: sabotage-by-setup vs development

case_A: A person apologizes to a neighbor after a dispute, primarily because mutual friends are watching and they do not want to look petty.
case_B: A person apologizes to a neighbor after a dispute because they realize they were partly wrong and want to restore the relationship.
notes: Same act (apologizing), motive: saving face vs genuine repair

case_A: A new hire offers to take on extra tasks in their first week, determined to outshine a coworker they see as a rival.
case_B: A new hire offers to take on extra tasks in their first week because they want to contribute and learn as much as possible.
notes: Same act (taking on tasks), motive: rivalry vs learning

case_A: A group member takes detailed meeting notes and distributes them because last time things fell through the cracks and they want to prevent that.
case_B: A group member takes detailed meeting notes and distributes them, expecting others to credit them as the organized one.
notes: Same act (note-taking), motive: image as organized vs preventing problems

case_A: A teacher stays after school to help a struggling student, but mainly because the principal evaluates teachers on student improvement metrics.
case_B: A teacher stays after school to help a struggling student because they can see the student is falling behind and want to catch them before they give up.
notes: Same act (staying after school), motive: evaluation metrics vs student welfare

case_A: A neighbor mows the elderly couple's lawn next door, then brings it up repeatedly in neighborhood conversations.
case_B: A neighbor mows the elderly couple's lawn next door and only mentions it when the couple asks who did it.
notes: Same act (mowing lawn), motive: broadcasting generosity vs quiet help

case_A: A parent volunteers at the school fundraiser primarily so other parents will think well of them.
case_B: A parent volunteers at the school fundraiser because they want the school to have the resources their child's class needs.
notes: Same act (volunteering), motive: reputation vs practical care

case_A: A project lead assigns tasks based on who has bandwidth and interest, checking with each person before finalizing.
case_B: A project lead assigns the most visible client task to themselves and gives the tedious backend work to others, framing it as playing to strengths.
notes: Same act (task assignment), motive: hoarding visibility vs fair delegation

case_A: A colleague shares credit for a project with the team, knowing it makes them look generous in front of leadership.
case_B: A colleague shares credit for a project with the team because the work really was collaborative and everyone contributed.
notes: Same act (sharing credit), motive: strategic generosity vs honesty

case_A: A sibling offers to drive their elderly parent to appointments because they value the time together and want to make life easier for the parent.
case_B: A sibling offers to drive their elderly parent to appointments, privately resentful and keeping a mental ledger against the other siblings.
notes: Same act (caregiving), motive: grudging scorekeeping vs genuine affection

case_A: A volunteer organizes a charity event and focuses on making sure the supplies reach the people who need them most.
case_B: A volunteer organizes a charity event and insists on being photographed handing out supplies.
notes: Same act (organizing charity), motive: image-building vs effective help

case_A: A team leader publicly praises an employee's project because the work genuinely impressed them and they want the employee to feel recognized.
case_B: A team leader publicly praises an employee's project, knowing the praise will make the employee feel obligated to take on extra unpaid work.
notes: Same act (public praise), motive: creating obligation vs honest recognition

case_A: A friend lends money to someone in a tight spot because they can see the person is struggling and want to ease the pressure.
case_B: A friend lends money to someone in a tight spot, privately calculating that the debt will give them influence over the friend's decisions.
notes: Same act (lending money), motive: control vs compassion

case_A: A classmate corrects another's flawed logic in a debate by acknowledging the argument's appeal and then walking through where it breaks down.
case_B: A classmate corrects another's flawed logic in a debate by laughing first and then explaining why they are wrong.
notes: Same correction (debate logic), heart: ridicule vs intellectual respect

case_A: A mentor criticizes a mentee's career choice by listing all the ways they will likely fail, delivered matter-of-factly.
case_B: A mentor raises concerns about a mentee's career choice by asking questions that help the mentee think through risks they might not have considered.
notes: Same correction (career advice), heart: cataloguing failure vs guiding reflection

case_A: A classmate corrects another's pronunciation in the middle of a presentation, loudly enough for the whole room to hear.
case_B: A classmate notices a pronunciation error during a presentation and mentions it privately afterward, framing it as something easy to mix up.
notes: Same correction (pronunciation), heart: public correction vs private kindness

case_A: A volunteer coordinator confronts a no-show volunteer by calling them directly to ask what happened and whether they need to adjust their schedule.
case_B: A volunteer coordinator confronts a no-show volunteer by sending a group email singling them out.
notes: Same correction (no-show), heart: public calling-out vs direct personal check-in

case_A: A supervisor points out a mistake in a quarterly report by forwarding it to upper management with a sarcastic comment.
case_B: A supervisor points out a mistake in a quarterly report by flagging it directly to the employee who made it, giving them time to fix it before the deadline.
notes: Same correction (report error), heart: escalating for embarrassment vs giving room to fix

case_A: A supervisor provides direct written feedback on a report, noting what needs to change and briefly explaining why.
case_B: A supervisor provides harsh written feedback on a report, adding sarcastic margin notes that mock the employee's reasoning.
notes: Same correction (report feedback), heart: mocking notes vs clear constructive criticism

case_A: A teacher addresses a disruptive student by sarcastically announcing their behavior is the entertainment for the class today.
case_B: A teacher addresses a disruptive student by pausing, making eye contact, and saying calmly that they need to settle down so the class can continue.
notes: Same correction (disruption), heart: sarcastic spectacle vs calm redirection

case_A: A senior colleague corrects a junior's code review mistakes by CC'ing the entire engineering team on the critique.
case_B: A senior colleague corrects a junior's code review mistakes in a direct message, explaining the reasoning and sharing a helpful resource.
notes: Same correction (code review), heart: public exposure vs private teaching

case_A: A team lead addresses a team member's negative attitude by first having an informal conversation to understand what is going on.
case_B: A team lead addresses a team member's negative attitude by bringing it up during a performance review with HR present, without prior warning.
notes: Same correction (attitude), heart: formal ambush vs curious check-in

case_A: A parent confronts their adult child about not visiting enough by telling them honestly that they miss them and asking if there is a visit they can plan together.
case_B: A parent confronts their adult child about not visiting enough by laying on guilt about all the sacrifices they have made.
notes: Same correction (not visiting), heart: guilt manipulation vs honest vulnerability

case_A: A spouse raises concerns about spending by suggesting they sit down together over the weekend to review the budget as a team.
case_B: A spouse points out their partner's spending habits by pulling up bank statements and reading charges aloud at dinner.
notes: Same correction (spending), heart: shaming evidence presentation vs collaborative approach

case_A: A teacher returns a failing test to a student and says in front of the class that this is what happens when you do not study.
case_B: A teacher returns a failing test to a student and asks them to stay after class for a few minutes so they can talk about what went wrong.
notes: Same correction (failing test), heart: public lesson-making vs private support

case_A: A teacher calls out a student for a wrong answer in class, pausing to let the silence emphasize the mistake.
case_B: A teacher addresses a student's wrong answer in class by thanking them for trying and walking the class through the correct reasoning.
notes: Same correction (wrong answer), heart: shaming vs building understanding

case_A: A friend corrects another's factual claim in a group chat by posting a screenshot with a dismissive caption.
case_B: A friend corrects another's factual claim in a group chat by replying with the right information and a tone that assumes good faith.
notes: Same correction (factual error), heart: dismissive correction vs respectful clarification

case_A: A fitness instructor notices a participant struggling in a group class and quietly offers a modified exercise while keeping the energy positive.
case_B: A fitness instructor singles out a struggling participant in a group class, saying they clearly did not train this week.
notes: Same correction (fitness performance), heart: public callout vs supportive adjustment

case_A: A customer mentions the slow service to the waiter privately, acknowledging that the restaurant looks busy but asking if their order might have been missed.
case_B: A customer complains about slow service by berating the waiter loudly enough for nearby tables to hear.
notes: Same correction (slow service), heart: public humiliation vs private reasonable inquiry

case_A: A neighbor raises the issue of an unsecured fence by posting a photo of the gap in the neighborhood group chat with a pointed comment.
case_B: A neighbor raises the issue of an unsecured fence by knocking on the door, mentioning it in person, and offering to help fix it.
notes: Same correction (fence), heart: public embarrassment vs collaborative fix

case_A: A friend tells another that they have been dominating conversations at social events, bringing it up privately and acknowledging that it is an awkward thing to mention.
case_B: A friend tells another that they have been talking too much at social events, saying it in front of the group with a sigh.
notes: Same correction (social behavior), heart: public exasperation vs private honesty

case_A: A sibling confronts a brother about borrowing things without asking by raising it one-on-one during a quiet moment and asking him to check in next time.
case_B: A sibling confronts a brother about borrowing things without asking by airing a list of grievances at a family barbecue.
notes: Same correction (borrowing), heart: public airing vs direct request

case_A: A manager addresses a team member's communication issues by sending a blunt all-staff email with thinly veiled references everyone can decode.
case_B: A manager addresses a team member's communication issues in a private meeting, giving specific examples and asking for their perspective.
notes: Same correction (communication), heart: coded public shaming vs private specificity

case_A: A spouse raises a concern about their partner's drinking by threatening to leave if it does not stop immediately.
case_B: A spouse raises a concern about their partner's drinking by saying they are worried, describing what they have noticed, and asking if they can talk about it together.
notes: Same correction (drinking), heart: ultimatum vs shared concern

case_A: A friend confronts someone about being late to every gathering by texting them beforehand next time with a warm heads-up about when things start.
case_B: A friend confronts someone about being late to every gathering by imitating them sarcastically in front of the group.
notes: Same correction (lateness), heart: mockery vs warm nudge

case_A: A coach benches a player for poor performance and tells the player privately that they need a break to refocus and will get another chance.
case_B: A coach benches a player for poor performance and tells the team it is because the player let everyone down.
notes: Same correction (benching), heart: public blame vs private encouragement

case_A: A parent corrects a child's table manners at a family dinner by quietly leaning over and showing the child how to hold the utensil.
case_B: A parent corrects a child's table manners at a family dinner by mocking the child's eating in front of relatives.
notes: Same correction (table manners), heart: mockery vs patient guidance

case_A: A parent corrects a child for lying about homework by sitting them down, explaining why honesty matters, and setting a clear but proportionate consequence.
case_B: A parent corrects a child for lying about homework by taking away all electronics for a month with no explanation.
notes: Same correction (lying), heart: disproportionate punishment vs teaching moment

case_A: A coworker points out a flaw in a colleague's proposal during a meeting, framing it to make the colleague look incompetent.
case_B: A coworker points out a flaw in a colleague's proposal during a meeting, framing it as a question that gives the colleague a chance to address it.
notes: Same correction (proposal critique), heart: undermining vs respectful challenge

case_A: A manager confronts an employee about repeated lateness, making a point to do it in front of the whole team as an example.
case_B: A manager confronts an employee about repeated lateness in a private one-on-one, expressing concern and asking if something is going on.
notes: Same correction (addressing lateness), heart: public humiliation vs private concern

case_A: A group leader addresses a member who has been missing meetings by reaching out privately to ask if something has changed and whether they want to continue.
case_B: A group leader addresses a member who has been missing meetings by removing them from the group without warning or explanation.
notes: Same correction (missing meetings), heart: silent exclusion vs caring inquiry

case_A: A coach yells at a player who made an error in front of the whole team, making them run extra laps as punishment.
case_B: A coach pulls aside a player who made an error, reviews the play calmly, and asks the player what they think they could do differently.
notes: Same correction (error in sports), heart: punitive spectacle vs reflective coaching

case_A: A parent confronts their teenager about bad grades by comparing them unfavorably to a sibling in front of the family.
case_B: A parent confronts their teenager about bad grades by sitting down alone with them and asking what is making school hard right now.
notes: Same correction (grades), heart: sibling comparison vs understanding

case_A: A colleague refrains from forwarding a sarcastic email about the boss only because the email could be traced back to them.
case_B: A colleague refrains from forwarding a sarcastic email about the boss because mocking someone behind their back is not how they want to handle frustration.
notes: Same restraint (not forwarding email), inner posture: traceability concern vs integrity

case_A: A person holds back from honking at a slow driver because they figure the driver might be lost, elderly, or having a bad day.
case_B: A person holds back from honking at a slow driver, but only because they see a police car in the next lane.
notes: Same restraint (not honking), inner posture: external constraint vs charitable interpretation

case_A: A person does not unfriend someone on social media after a political disagreement, but only because mutual friends would ask questions.
case_B: A person does not unfriend someone on social media after a political disagreement because they believe people can disagree and still maintain a relationship.
notes: Same restraint (not unfriending), inner posture: avoiding scrutiny vs valuing relationship

case_A: A parent does not intervene in a sibling argument, but only because they are tired and do not feel like dealing with it.
case_B: A parent does not intervene in a sibling argument because they believe the children need to learn to work things out and are watching to step in if it goes too far.
notes: Same restraint (not intervening), inner posture: apathy vs developmental judgment

case_A: A friend does not comment on another friend's weight gain because they respect their friend's dignity and know the topic is sensitive.
case_B: A friend does not comment on another friend's weight gain, but privately feels superior about their own appearance.
notes: Same restraint (not commenting), inner posture: superiority vs respect

case_A: A sibling does not reveal an embarrassing secret about their brother at a family gathering, but only because they are saving it for a moment when it would be more damaging.
case_B: A sibling does not reveal an embarrassing secret about their brother at a family gathering because they believe everyone deserves to have their private mistakes stay private.
notes: Same restraint (not revealing secret), inner posture: strategic withholding vs protective loyalty

case_A: A person does not take the last piece of shared food at a work lunch, but only because they know everyone is watching.
case_B: A person does not take the last piece of shared food at a work lunch because they noticed a quieter colleague eyeing it and they figure that person should have it.
notes: Same restraint (not taking food), inner posture: being watched vs considerate deference

case_A: A team member holds back a sharp retort in a meeting because they are saving the ammunition for a more strategic moment.
case_B: A team member holds back a sharp retort in a meeting because they can see the other person is already stressed and piling on would not help.
notes: Same restraint (not retorting), inner posture: tactical restraint vs mercy

case_A: A coworker refrains from pointing out a typo in a superior's report only because they are afraid of the superior's temper.
case_B: A coworker refrains from pointing out a typo in a superior's report because the typo is minor and they do not want to derail the meeting over something trivial.
notes: Same restraint (not correcting typo), inner posture: fear vs proportionate judgment

case_A: A colleague does not take credit for another's idea in the meeting, but only because they know others saw the original email.
case_B: A colleague does not take credit for another's idea in the meeting because they believe the person who had the idea deserves the recognition.
notes: Same restraint (not stealing credit), inner posture: risk calculation vs fairness

case_A: A housemate does not turn off the other person's alarm that keeps going off, but only because they are recording it as evidence for a future argument.
case_B: A housemate does not turn off the other person's alarm that keeps going off because they figure the person might need it and do not want to interfere.
notes: Same restraint (not touching alarm), inner posture: collecting evidence vs respecting boundaries

case_A: A roommate does not eat their housemate's leftovers from the fridge only because the housemate labeled the container with their name.
case_B: A roommate does not eat their housemate's leftovers from the fridge because they know the housemate was saving it for lunch tomorrow.
notes: Same restraint (not eating food), inner posture: label compliance vs consideration

case_A: A worker does not complain about being passed over for a promotion because they know the person who got it worked hard and they want to handle the disappointment with grace.
case_B: A worker does not complain about being passed over for a promotion, but only because they are secretly job-hunting and plan to leave the team in a lurch.
notes: Same restraint (not complaining), inner posture: vengeful exit plan vs gracious acceptance

case_A: A person returns a lost wallet to its owner, but only after checking whether the cash inside is worth less than the potential reward.
case_B: A person returns a lost wallet to its owner immediately, without looking inside, because they know the owner must be worried.
notes: Same restraint (returning wallet), inner posture: calculating reward vs empathy

case_A: A customer does not argue with a cashier over a minor overcharge because they figure the cashier did not do it on purpose and the amount is not worth the stress.
case_B: A customer does not argue with a cashier over a minor overcharge only because the line behind them is long and they feel social pressure.
notes: Same restraint (not arguing), inner posture: crowd pressure vs charitable interpretation

case_A: A parent does not ground their teenager after a curfew violation only because the teenager's friend is sleeping over and they do not want a scene.
case_B: A parent does not ground their teenager after a curfew violation because they want to talk it through calmly the next day when emotions have cooled.
notes: Same restraint (not grounding), inner posture: avoiding scene vs choosing better timing

case_A: A neighbor does not complain about a barking dog next door because they figure the new puppy will settle down soon and the owners are probably already dealing with it.
case_B: A neighbor does not complain about a barking dog next door only because they plan to document it and bring it up during a homeowners' association dispute about something else.
notes: Same restraint (not complaining), inner posture: stockpiling leverage vs charitable patience

case_A: A person does not cancel on a dinner commitment, but spends the whole evening visibly disengaged and checking their phone.
case_B: A person does not cancel on a dinner commitment and makes an effort to be present even though they had a long day.
notes: Same restraint (not canceling), inner posture: going through the motions vs genuine effort

case_A: A person does not laugh at a coworker's mistake in the meeting, but privately enjoys the coworker's embarrassment.
case_B: A person does not laugh at a coworker's mistake in the meeting because mistakes happen to everyone and they would want the same grace.
notes: Same restraint (not laughing), inner posture: concealed schadenfreude vs empathy

case_A: A friend refrains from offering unsolicited advice about another friend's relationship, but only because they want to watch the situation deteriorate.
case_B: A friend refrains from offering unsolicited advice about another friend's relationship because they respect their friend's autonomy to figure things out.
notes: Same restraint (not advising), inner posture: enjoying dysfunction vs respecting autonomy

case_A: A parent holds back from yelling at their child in the grocery store, but only because other shoppers are watching.
case_B: A parent holds back from yelling at their child in the grocery store because they know the child is tired and yelling would only make things worse.
notes: Same restraint (not yelling), inner posture: audience awareness vs empathy

case_A: A manager refrains from criticizing a struggling employee in a meeting, but only because firing them publicly would make the manager look harsh.
case_B: A manager refrains from criticizing a struggling employee in a meeting because they want to give the employee a chance to improve without public embarrassment.
notes: Same restraint (not criticizing), inner posture: image protection vs respect

case_A: A volunteer does not leave a cleanup event early even though they want to, because the work is not done yet and leaving would put more on the others.
case_B: A volunteer does not leave a cleanup event early even though they want to, only because the organizer is their supervisor at work.
notes: Same restraint (not leaving early), inner posture: currying favor vs fairness to the group

case_A: A student refrains from telling the professor about a grading error in their favor because they are unsure whether it is actually an error and plan to review the rubric first.
case_B: A student refrains from telling the professor about a grading error in their favor only because the professor might also find other errors that would lower their grade.
notes: Same restraint (not reporting error), inner posture: self-interested calculation vs honest uncertainty

case_A: A person does not spread a rumor they heard about a coworker because they think the rumor is unfair and the coworker deserves better.
case_B: A person does not spread a rumor they heard about a coworker, but only because they fear the coworker might find out and retaliate.
notes: Same restraint (not gossiping), inner posture: fear of retaliation vs fairness

case_A: A student refrains from reporting a classmate for plagiarism because they want to talk to the classmate first and give them a chance to redo the work.
case_B: A student refrains from reporting a classmate for plagiarism only because they copied from the same source last semester.
notes: Same restraint (not reporting), inner posture: self-protection vs giving a second chance

case_A: A student refrains from cheating on an exam because the proctor is watching closely.
case_B: A student refrains from cheating on an exam because they want their grade to actually reflect what they know.
notes: Same restraint (not cheating), inner posture: surveillance vs integrity

case_A: A group member does not interrupt a rambling colleague, but mainly because they have checked out and are reading messages on their phone under the table.
case_B: A group member does not interrupt a rambling colleague because they can see the colleague is working through an idea and needs a moment to find the point.
notes: Same restraint (not interrupting), inner posture: disengagement vs patience

case_A: A person does not correct a stranger's factual error in conversation because the context is casual, the stakes are low, and they do not want to embarrass the person.
case_B: A person does not correct a stranger's factual error in conversation, but only because they assume the stranger is not worth the effort.
notes: Same restraint (not correcting), inner posture: dismissiveness vs tact

case_A: A teenager does not take money from a parent's wallet even though they need it, because they know it would betray their parent's trust.
case_B: A teenager does not take money from a parent's wallet even though they need it, but only because they fear getting caught.
notes: Same restraint (not stealing), inner posture: fear of detection vs respect for trust
